coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of oil-soluble carbon dots via pyrolysis and their diverse applications in doxycycline detection, fluorescent ink and film
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yuexia
Fan, Xiaopeng
Sun, Xuansen
Yang, Xintong
Li, Zhongping
Yang, Zhenhua
Dong, Chuan
coredata.dc:description: The overuse of doxycycline poses a risk for ecological environment. Advanced materials such as anti-counterfeiting and photovoltaic materials are urgently needed to develop innovative strategies for exploiting solar cells and protecting valuable products. Herein, oil-soluble CDs (o-CDs) were successfully fabricated from citric acid, tris-base and oleylamine as precursors via pyrolysis method. The o-CDs with uniform size distribution exhibited a high quantum yield of 0.48 and excellent photostability. The fluorescence of o-CDs was rapidly quenched by doxycycline at room temperature without further modification. Optimal conditions were selected to construct a fluorescence probe with high selectivity and good sensitivity to detect doxycycline. Interestingly, the probe achieved two linear ranges of 0.85––16.7 µM and 16.7––33.4 µM with a low detection limit of 0.26 µM. Furthermore, inner filter effect (IFE) was dominated in the process in which doxycycline interact with the oxygen-containing groups of o-CDs. This sensing platform has been further successfully applied to the detection of doxycycline in milk with recovery rates of 96.8%- 102.7% and relative standard deviations of 0.98%- 1.02%, suggesting that the novel probe has the potential to be applied in real samples. Moreover, o-CDs directly serve as fluorescence ink and work as fluorescence film using PVA as matrix because of strong fluorescence in the solid state, indicating that they have potential applications in anti-counterfeiting and photovoltaic materials. This is the first report that oil-soluble CDs via pyrolysis is applied in the detection of doxycycline in milk. Importantly, this work provides efficient strategies for the construction of anti-counterfeiting and photovoltaic materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Pyrolysis
Doxycycline
Fluorescence ink
Fluorescence film

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Calculation of the fluorescence quantum yield
Preparation and optical properties of fluorescent film
Morphological observation and structural characterization of as-fabricated o-CDs
Detection of doxycycline in milk sample

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation for Young Scientists of Shanxi Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Financial supports from the Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (Nos. 2020-002 and 2021-014 ) and Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province (No. 201801D221061 ) are gratefully acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images. (b) Particle size distribution. (c) FTIR diagram of o-CDs. (d) XPS full spectra. (e) C1s. (f) N1s and (g) O1s of o-CDs.
(a) Absorption, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the o-CDs. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of o-CDs when excited from 300 nm to 400 nm and (c) Photostability of the o-CDs.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of o-CDs with the increase of doxycycline concentration (excited at 370 nm). (b) Plot of 1/(F0-F) against 1/Cdoxycycline. (c) Effect of pH on fluorescence intensity of CDs with or without doxycycline. (d) Effect of reaction time on fluorescence intensity of CDs in the presence of different concentrations of doxycycline (0–100 µM). (e) Linear relationship between fluorescence response and doxycycline concentration. (f) Selective detection for DOX using the o-CDs sensor in the presence of various of antibiotics. (g) The fluorescence sensing platform for the detection of doxycycline.
(a) UV–vis spectra of CDs with and without 5 mM of doxycycline. (b) Fitting graph of o-CDs fluorescence lifetime.
Photographs of different patterns under daylight (a, b) and UV irradiation at 365 nm (c, d) on filter paper from o-CDs as ink (a, c- “山西大学 SXU”, b, d- the twelve Chinese zodiac signs).

(a) Photographs of PVA and PVA/o-CDs under D65 light and UV irradiation at 365 nm. (b) CIE diagram. (c) PL excitation and emission spectra of the PVA/o-CDs film. (d) PL spectra of the PVA/o-CDs film at different excitation wavelengths from 300 to 400 nm.
A schematic illustration of the synthesis of o-CDs.
Detection of doxycycline in milk samples.
Comparison of several methods for the detection of doxycycline.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of oil-soluble carbon dots via pyrolysis and their diverse applications in doxycycline detection, fluorescent ink and film

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Shanxi University, Wucheng Road 92#, Taiyuan 030006, Shanxi Province, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The overuse of doxycycline poses a risk for ecological environment. Advanced materials such as anti-counterfeiting and photovoltaic materials are urgently needed to develop innovative strategies for exploiting solar cells and protecting valuable products. Herein, oil-soluble CDs (o-CDs) were successfully fabricated from citric acid, tris-base and oleylamine as precursors via pyrolysis method. The o-CDs with uniform size distribution exhibited a high quantum yield of 0.48 and excellent photostability. The fluorescence of o-CDs was rapidly quenched by doxycycline at room temperature without further modification. Optimal conditions were selected to construct a fluorescence probe with high selectivity and good sensitivity to detect doxycycline. Interestingly, the probe achieved two linear ranges of 0.85––16.7 µM and 16.7––33.4 µM with a low detection limit of 0.26 µM. Furthermore, inner filter effect (IFE) was dominated in the process in which doxycycline interact with the oxygen-containing groups of o-CDs. This sensing platform has been further successfully applied to the detection of doxycycline in milk with recovery rates of 96.8%- 102.7% and relative standard deviations of 0.98%- 1.02%, suggesting that the novel probe has the potential to be applied in real samples. Moreover, o-CDs directly serve as fluorescence ink and work as fluorescence film using PVA as matrix because of strong fluorescence in the solid state, indicating that they have potential applications in anti-counterfeiting and photovoltaic materials. This is the first report that oil-soluble CDs via pyrolysis is applied in the detection of doxycycline in milk. Importantly, this work provides efficient strategies for the construction of anti-counterfeiting and photovoltaic materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The tetracycline antibiotics have been widely used as supplements in animal feed to promote animal growth and prevent various diseases [1]. Doxycycline, as a second-generation tetracycline derivative, is adopted to treat bacterial infections including respiratory tract infections, urinary infections and biliary tracts [2,3]; its anti-inflammation and anti-apoptotic effects are also identified [4]. Unfortunately, the drug is not fully absorbed, and consequently released into water, soil and food environment [5]. The abuse of doxycycline leads to an unsafe residue level in the animal [2,6] and consequently transferred into food samples. Excess doxycycline induces antibiotic resistance [7]. The intake of doxycycline-contaminated animal-based foods leads to unintentional bioaccumulation of doxycycline that cause profound effects on human health such as liver injury, gastric perforation, dental staining and cardiac problems [4]. The negative effects pf doxycycline on human health, to some degree, depend on the dosage. Thus, it is critical to develop a reliable and accurate assay to detect doxycycline levels in food.

Recently, much efforts have been focused on detecting doxycycline using numerous conventional techniques including electrochemical analysis, liquid chromatography and flow-injection analysis, have been developed to monitor doxycycline [8–10]. However, these methods commonly suffer from low sensitivity and/or complicated preparation. Fluorescence sensing method is superior among these methods since it is sensitive, fast and cost-effective [11,12]. Carbon dots (CDs), a new type of carbon materials, are gaining more and more interest because of many advantages such as simple synthesis, low toxicity, high quantum yield and good biocompatibility. Huang et al. [13] synthesized a blue-fluorescence nanodots using hydrothermal reaction to detect doxycycline. Metal and nonmetal-doped strategies are proposed to improve the optical properties of fluorescence probe [14,15]. A Fe-doped CDs by hydrothermal synthesis was applied in doxycycline detection [16]. Xue et al prepared fluorescent S, N-CDs by the same method to analyze the concentration of doxycycline in living cell [7]. However, hydrothermal, solvothermal and electrochemical methods to prepare CDs need to be carried out under liquid phase environment and/or high-pressure condition [17]; thisis veryunfavorable for large-scale production, and even cause hidden dangers. Instead, free-solvent pyrolysis is much safer and more efficient for CDs synthesis to achieve thermal decomposition of wide-ranging raw materials [18–21]. Pyrolytic synthesis is widely recognized as eco-friendly synthesis methods for preparing CDs due to their easy accessibility to produce high purity with high yield [22]. There are few works about pyrolysis-based CDs probe for the detection of tetracycline.

Currently, carbon-focused photovoltaic and solar cells seem to a promising industry [23]. Surprisingly, CDs play a very critical role in the fabrication of photovoltaic devices because of their excellent optoelectronic properties (bandgap tunability, excellent photostability and strong photoluminescence) [24]. Furthermore, anti-counterfeiting materials have gained greater attention in several domains, such as logical gates, sensors, and security warnings [25]. Since o-CDs are excellent optical material, it is very necessary to exploit new photovoltaic material and an anti-counterfeiting method based optical method.

The fluorescent CDs can be expected by employing citric acid as a carbon source. Gu et al synthesized a N-doped CDs with citric acid as carbon and urea as nitrogen source via hydrothermal reaction [26]. In this work, oil-soluble CDs (o-CDs) were achieved via pyrolysis using citric acid as carbon source and tris-base as nitrogen source; oleylamine was also used as another precursor to improve the hydrophobicity of CDs surface (Scheme 1 ). The long alkyl chain of olylamines, as a non-polar hydrophobic group, reduces the polarity of the CDs and makes the CDs change from water soluble to oil soluble, enabling the controlled synthesis of oil soluble CDs. A novel fluorescence sensing method was successfully achieved based on fluorescence quenching of o-CDs by doxycycline. This is the first report that oil-soluble CDs via pyrolysis is applied in the detection of doxycycline. Also, o-CDs can be utilized as fluorescent inks and fluorescent flexible films. Overall, these findings show that the O-CDs have potential applications in detecting doxycycline, anti-counterfeiting, and photovoltaic materials.

Oleylamine was obtained from JSC Chemical technology Company (Guangzhou, China). Sodium hydroxide was purchased from Tianjin Continental Chemical Reagent Company (Tianjin, China). Other chemical materials were supplied from Aladdin Chemistry Company (Shanghai, China). All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA, molecular weight: 1500). Distilled water was used for the preparation of all aqueous solutions.

o-CDs were prepared by pyrolysis of citric acid, tris-base and oleylamine as precursors. Initial experiments focused on exploring the optimal concentration of several precursors for the synthesis of o-CDs, reaction temperature and reaction time. A series of mixture including citric acid (2.0 g), different concentrations of tris-base (0.1, 0.20, 0.25, 0.3, 0.35, 0.40 g) were heated in a 100 mL-breaker. Afterwards, oleylamine (0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9, 1.0 mL) was added into the flask and heated for 1 min. The resulting o-CDs was dispersed into DMSO solvent and stored at 4 °C for further use. In the subsequent experiments, the solvent of CDs solution was DMSO.

The sizes, morphologies and surface functional properties of o-CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) with a JEM-2100 (JEOL, Japan), AXIS ULTRA DLD X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Kratos, Japan) and Bruker Tensorll Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) (BRUKER, German). The fluorescence lifetimes were determined by FLS920 (Edinburgh, UK). All fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded at a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent, USA) and a Lambda 365 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, USA), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of the fluorescence quantum yield
ce:section.ce:para: The quantum yield of the as-prepared o-CDs was determined according to the method in the previous report [27]. Quinine sulfate in 0.1 M sulfuric acid solution was chosen as a fluorescence standard sample.

In a typical detection procedure, as-prepared o-CDs was added to 3 mL DMSO to give a final o-CDs concentration of 0.125 g/mL. The optimal conditions focused on different pH values and different reaction time for detecting doxycycline. All experiments were carried out in triplicate. To verify the selectivity and sensitivity of CDs probe for doxycycline detection, the effects of other antibiotics (The antibiotic solution at a concentration of 0.01 M was prepared using water as the solvent) on fluorescence intensity of CDs were investigated.

The detection limit of the method was calculated according to the formula (3σ/k), where σ is the standard deviation of blank measurements (n = 11) and the slope was obtained from the fitting equation.

The milk sample was obtained from the local shop in Shanxi University and prepared following the procedure. Four aliquots of 1.0 mL of acetonitrile were gradually added into 2.0 mL of the milk and mixed in a shaker for 15 min. Then, the milk was mixed with 1.0 g NaCl for another 15 min. The resulting milk sample was centrifuged for 5 min at 3000 rpm at 4 °C to remove any deposits. The supernatant was spiked with different concentrations of doxycycline. The standard addition recovery method was adopted to measure the doxycycline in natural samples and triplicate measurements were performed for each sample.

The o-CDs solution was transferred into an ink marking pen-tube without any pretreatment. We wrote text in Chinese “山西大学” and its English abbreviation “sxu” on filter paper. Furthermore, the twelve Chinese zodiac signs seal was dipped in o-CDs solution and imprinted on filter paper. After drying of these letters and pictures in the open air, they were subjected to UV irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and optical properties of fluorescent film
ce:section.ce:para: 3 g of PVA was added into 30 mL of ultrapure water and stirred vigorously at 85 °C until complete dissolution. Afterwards, the PVA solution was mixed with 3 mL of o-CDs solution. Finally, the obtained PVA/o-CDs composite was transferred to a clean Petri dish and dried at 45 °C for 12 h. The fluorescent films were obtained. Fluorescence spectra of CDs/PVA film were recorded at a F-7100 fluorescence spectrophotometer (HITACHI, Japan).

The overall o-CDs synthesis is shown in Scheme 1. The o-CDs were synthesized via pyrolysis from citric acid, tris-base and oleylamine. Since temperature and reaction time affect the carbonization of the precursors and functionalization of o-CDs surface, a series of experiments were performed to achieve highly photoluminescent o-CDs. As shown in Fig. S1, when the ratio of citric acid/tris-base is set as 5:1, the temperature is set as 190 °C and reactive time is 9 min, strong photoluminescence (PL) of o-CDs is observed (Fig. S1 a). The as-prepared o-CDs exhibits stronger photoluminescence as oleylamine is 0.3 mL and reaction time of oleylamine is 1 min (Fig. S1b). Further increase of reaction time induces a sharp drop because higher temperature and more time do not facilitate the carbonization of o-CDs [28]. The strong FL intensity of o-dots may stem from the nitrogen doped into carbon core, forming the derivatives of citrazinic acid [29]. As previously reported by Schneider [29], citric acid can undergo intramolecular condensation and cyclization with tris-base and oleylamine to form derivatives of citrazinic acid. More likely, carboxylic functionalities from citric acid may react with the terminal from tris-base, leading to the formation of networks linked by ester bonds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological observation and structural characterization of as-fabricated o-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology, functional group analysis and chemical compositions of the N-doped o-CDs were further characterized by TEM technology, FTIR and XPS. Fig. 1 shows that the as-prepared o-CDs appear to be spherical nanoparticles with an average diameter of about 2.33 ± 0.67 nm. Because of interaction of oleylamine, o-CDs shows superior dispersibility in DMSO. To obtain structural information of the o-CDs, the FTIR spectrum is shown in Fig. 1c. The FTIR spectrum of o-CDs displays an absorption at 3443 cm−1, which is attributed to the OH/NH stretching vibration. Two peaks at 3139 cm−1 and 1402 cm−1 respond to the stretching vibration of CH and CN, respectively, while the absorption bands at 1732, 1199 and 1545 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibration of CO and CO [30], bending vibration of NO/CC, respectively [16], thus providing evidence for the possession of COOH group.

The XPS full spectrum clearly reveals three predominant peaks at 282 eV, 399 eV and 530 eV, which result from C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s signals, respectively (Fig. 1d). The contents of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen account for 67.2%, 29.3% and 3.5%, respectively. The relatively high C content is consistent with the high carbonization degree. The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1e) shows the presence of sp2 at 284.8 eV (CC/CC), 286.7 eV (CH), and 288.93 eV(CO). The high-resolution N 1 s spectrum of CDs (Fig. 1f) displays two main peaks: CNC (401.0 eV) and OCN (401.9 eV). Moreover, the O 1 s spectrum of o-CDs (Fig. 1g) corresponds to 532.1 eV (CO) and COC (533.4 eV). From what has been discussed above, these results are consistent with those of FTIR, confirming that the o-CDs possess abundant COOH and NH groups on their surface and suffered from cyclization reaction citric acid and tris-base.

The optical properties of the o-CDs were further explored by the UV–vis and fluorescence steady-state emission spectra in DMSO solution (Fig. 2 ). As shown in Fig. 2a, the absorption spectrum exhibits a broad absorption peak located around 358 nm, which is assigned to n -π* transition at the edge of carbon lattice [31] and surface states associated with functional surface groups [29]. The surface state leads to trapping of excited state energy, which gives rise to strong emission [32]. The maximum PL emission peak excited at 370 nm is centered at 421 nm, corresponding to blue fluorescence. This is consistent with UV-irradiated emission results. Additionally, maximum emission wavelength did not change over the entire excitation range from 300 nm to 400 nm (Fig. 2b), suggestive of the excitation-independent characteristic; this feature reveals the dominance of a single emissive transition [33]. Additionally, the wide particle size distribution range and independent emission peaks indicate that the fluorescence behavior of N-doped o-CDs is not affected by the size effect. Under UV-light irradiation, the o-CDs suspended in DMSO show intense bluish fluorescence emission (Fig. 2a-insert). The fluorescent quantum yield of o-CDs was calculated to be 0.48 following the reported procedure by using quinine sulfate as a reference. This obtained quantum yield is superior to the previous o-CDs reported by Dong et al.[34]. Since a linear relation between fluorescence quantum yield and lifetime is very common, the radiative constant rate is calculated to be 4.5 × 107 s−1, comparable with the commercial quantum dots [35]. This suggests that o-CDs are promising candidate in electronical applications.

Photostability of the o-CDs is critical for the application of fluorescent materials. The related experiments of o-CDs were tested under 3 h continuous irradiation using a xenon lamp. As shown in Fig. 2c, there is barely any recognizable change in the PL intensity after UV irradiation for 90 min, revealing that the as-prepared o-CDs are highly photostable, without photobleaching effect observed. Consequently, as-prepared o-CDs could have excellent potential for technologically relevant systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of doxycycline in milk sample
ce:section.ce:para: Since the o-CDs obtained by the solvent-free pyrolysis have good optical properties and excellent photostability (Fig. 2), the PL properties of o-CDs probe were explored to examine the sensitivity of o-CDs-based fluorescence sensing platform. Fig. 3 a shows the maximum emission peaks of doxycycline at 421 nm. Fluorescence quenching is observed as its concentration is increased from 0 to 33.4 µM. The association constant was determined to confirm the binding affinity of doxycycline to o-CDs using the Benesi-Hildebrand equation [36]. By fitting the PL intensity into Benesi-Hildebrand equation (Fig. 3b), the binding constant was calculated to be 2.04 × 104 M−1, verifying high affinity of doxycycline to o-CDs.

To validate the stability of this quantification method, the impact of pH on its fluorescence intensity was examined. A gradient PL intensity change of o-CDs with pH and reaction time is shown in Fig. 3c, d. In the current study, we used the Britton Robinson (BR) buffer solution, which was consisted of 0.04 mol/L phosphoric acid, boric acid, acetic acid and 0.2 mol/L NaOH solution, and the solvents were DMSO. The PL intensity fluctuates little in the range of pH 2–9 and maximizes at pH 6. The PL intensity decreases with pH in the range of 9 to 11 (Fig. 3c). It is suspected that alkaline state might induce dissociation of fluorescence complex. Fig. 3d shows that the selected reaction time almost does not affect fluorescence intensity of the CDs within 2–12 min, thus reaction time of 2 min was used in subsequent experiment, while keeping the pH constant at 6, suggesting that CDs rapidly form the fluorescence complex with doxycycline hydrochloride under experiment conditions.

The quantitative analysis was performed by establishing the calibration curve with different concentrations of doxycycline. The Stern-Volmer plot of ΔF values versus concentration of doxycycline is shown in Fig. 3f. Here, the fluorescence intensity exhibits an excellent linear relationship associated with doxycycline in the range of between 0 and 16.7 µM with the correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.99062. Moreover, the LOD for the determination of doxycycline is calculated to be 0.26 μM using 3σ/s formula (σ is the standard deviation of 11 blank measurements, and s is the slope of the calibration curve). An excellent linear relationship associated with doxycycline between 16.7 and 33.4 µM is achieved with the correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.0.99197. Detection limit was also found to be 0.43 µM.

These o-CDs are promising candidates for the detection of antibiotics in the environment. The complex compositions of natural food samples pose a great challenge to the detection methods not only in sensitivity but also more significantly in selectivity [37]. Other antibiotics that may be present in the organism and food were selected as the interferents, antibiotic solutions with a concentration of 0.01 M were prepared with water as the solvent and their effect on fluorescence intensity was investigated (Fig. 3f). The FL signal of doxycycline is significantly lower than other antibiotics (P < 0.001), indicating the interferences of these antibiotics are negligible. Therefore, the o-CDs can be applied for doxycycline detection as fluorescent probe (Fig. 3g).

Doxycycline residue is often found in a variety of food and medicine. Doxycycline analysis was checked in raw milk as available in the market to verify the accuracy and feasibility of the o-CDs fluorescence probe. The pretreated milk was spiked with different concentrations of doxycycline (3.34, 6.68, 10.02, 20.04, 23.38 and 26.72 µM) in the presence of o-CDs. As summarized in Table 1 , the relative standard deviation (RSD) is located between 0.98% and 1.02%. And the spike recoveries are in the range of 96.8% and 102.7%, indicating that the o-CDs fluorescence probe has excellent accuracy and reliability in the detection of doxycycline in real samples. The relative low detection limit, good accuracy and reliability broadens the application of CDs as a fluorescence probe for detecting doxycycline.
Based on the previous reports, Table 2 exhibits other sensing platforms for the detection of doxycycline. The performance of our CDs is among the best.

Generally, the decrease of the fluorescence is related to the energy transfer, inner filter effect (IFE) and dynamic and static quenching [42,43]. Both static quenching and PET change the fluorescent signal by forming a compound between CDs and quencher. The absorption band of doxycycline has a broad absorption at 364 nm, overlapping with the excitation spectrum of o-CDs; this feature suggests that the PL intensity can be suppressed by doxycycline due to IFE. Fig. 4 a shows an effective spectral overlap between the excitation band of o-CDs-doxycycline mixture and absorption band of doxycycline, indicating that the fluorescence quenching might be, to some degree, attributed to IFE. Fluorescence lifetime is characteristic for each fluorescent CDs. To further verify the mechanism of fluorescence probe for the detection of doxycycline, fluorescence lifetime of the o-CDs with varying concentrations of doxycycline was examined. Double-exponential decays (τ1 and τ2) are observed by deconvolution of the fluorescence decay measured excited at 370 nm (Fig. 4b and Table S1). Almost unchanged fluorescence lifetime and the absence of a new absorption confirmed that IFE contributes to the o-CDs towards detection of doxycycline. Furthermore, it is undeniable that the active groups from doxycycline interact with the COOH and NH group on the surface of o-CDs, which might result in the fluorescence quenching of o-CDs.

Nowadays, it is urgent to develop a new anti-counterfeiting strategy to distinguish between genuine and fake products [44]. Since writing ink displays distinct optical properties to the naked eye under daylight and UV light, it is difficult to be forged [45]. In the current study, the application of o-CDs as fluorescence ink was explored because of the excellent optical feature. Fig. 5 shows the designable patterns of the as-prepared o-CDs inks deposited on filter paper. Under the daylight condition, there is no visible pattern on the filter paper to the naked eye (Fig. 5a, b), Strikingly, the handwritten text of “山西大学 sxu” and “the twelve Chinese zodiac signs” clearly reappear on the filter paper when excited by a 365 nm UV light source (Fig. 5c, d). These characteristics of o-CDs-based ink signifies their huge potential in the areas of security and anti-counterfeiting. The applications will benefit from facile synthesis and cheap cost.

o-CDs are dispersed into PVA to prepare the PVA/o-CDscomposite film. The photographs of PVA/o-CDs film display outstanding transparency and pliability (Fig.S2). Under Artificial Daylight 6500 K (D65) light, PVA film shows the color of the filter paper itself while PVA/o-CDs film appears to be light yellow. When 365 nm UV-light was turned on, only PVA/o-CDs layer emits a blue-color fluorescence (Fig. 6 a). The chromaticity diagram (CIE) coordinates of the PVA film and PVA/o-CDs film under D65 light and 365 nm UV-light are displayed in Fig. 6b. The CIE coordinates of PVA in D65 light and 365 nm UV-light are (0.310, 0.306) and (0.257, 0.264), respectively; PVA/o-CDs film in D65 light and 365 nm UV-light exhibit CIE coordinates (0.328, 0.348) and (0.183, 0.133). The CIE was measured using color luminance meter (CS-150, Konica Minolta, Japan). The maximum PL emission peak of PVA/o-CDs in the solid state excited at 370 nm is centered at 418 nm, corresponding to blue fluorescence (Fig. 6c). This is consistent with UV-irradiated emission results. Additionally, maximum emission wavelength did not change over the entire excitation range from 300 nm to 380 nm (Fig. 6d), suggestive of the excitation-independent characteristic; this was consistent with the results of o-CDs fluorescence spectrum. Due to the excellent optoelectronic properties, PVA/o-CDs film can serve as a new type of photovoltaic material in the fabrication of photovoltaic devices such as solid-state solar cells, further expanding the application of o-CDs as photovoltaic materials.

The o-CDs emitting blue light are successfully synthesized by free-solvent pyrolysis method. The resulting o-CDs is dispersed in organic solvents to form clear solution with blue-emission fluorescence. The prepared o-CDs possess excellent optical properties with a relative high quantum yield and good photostability. o-CDs are successfully applied in diverse fields including fluorescence probe, fluorescence ink and film. Among various antibiotics, doxycycline can remarkably quench the fluorescence of o-CDs based on IFE. The feature renders the o-CDs to be used as fluorescence probe for detecting doxycycline. Moreover, the probe has wide linear range and low detection limit. The method was successfully applied in the detection for doxycycline in milk. The accuracy and spike recoveries are satisfactory. Moreover, the different optical features in D65 light and UV light and excellent fluorescence of PVA/o-CDs in the solid state pave the way for o-CDs application in the fields of anti-counterfeiting and photovoltaic products.

Yuexia Zhang: Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Writing – original draft. Xiaopeng Fan: Investigation. Xuansen Sun: Investigation. Xintong Yang: . Zhongping Li: Methodology. Zhenhua Yang: Writing – review & editing. Chuan Dong: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial supports from the Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (Nos. 2020-002 and 2021-014) and Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province (No. 201801D221061) are gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.123406.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Antibiotic residues in food: Extraction, analysis, and human health concerns
J. Agric. Food Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Chen, G.G. Ying, W.J. Deng, Antibiotic Residues in Food: Extraction, Analysis, and Human Health Concerns, Journal of agricultural and food chemistry, 67 (2019) 7569-7586.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Distribution of antibiotic, heavy metals and antibiotic resistance genes in livestock and poultry feces from different scale of farms in ningxia, China
J. Hazardous Mater.
